metadata.title: Why 'Looks Good' Fails as a Gate and What to Write Instead

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa, Liam — in for Bear. Three days later a student emailed: the syllabus link does not work? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Three days later a student emailed: the syllabus link does not work?

beats.narration_text: The teacher reviewed the new About page. It rendered. The nav link was there. The CSS looked right. They pushed. Three days later a student emailed: the syllabus link does not work.

card.copy: Why 'looks good' fails as a gate — and what to write instead

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] looks good fails as a gate

beats.narration_text: The page existed. The link existed. It pointed to syllabus.html. Correct relative to the project root. Wrong relative to where the school server served the page. The link had worked on local dev. It did not work in production. The condition that would have caught it was never written.

production_viz.label: local dev passes; school server fails — relative path mismatch
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: left 'local dev' with a teal check on the link; right 'school server' with a crimson X on the same link; a path label showing syllabus.html (relative) vs /syllabus.html (absolute)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] local dev passes / school server fails

beats.narration_text: Here is the question. Reviewing the output visually and verifying exit zero should be sufficient to catch link failures before deployment. Here is the case where a link passed both checks and failed in production. Why?

card.copy: Exit 0. Looks good. Link broken three days later. Why?

beats.narration_text: Looks good is a feeling, not a condition. A feeling can approve a build that silently fails three days later. A handoff condition has three properties. Specific: names the artifact and the property. Testable: verifiable by a command or explicit visual check. Binary: pass or fail. Looks good fails all three.

production_viz.label: three properties: specific, testable, binary — looks good fails all
production_viz.mechanic: three rows drawing in: Specific (teal check), Testable (teal check), Binary (teal check); then a second column 'looks good' with crimson X on each row

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three properties vs looks good — all fail

beats.narration_text: The weak condition: looks good. It does not name the artifact. It does not name the property being checked. It cannot be run. It has no threshold. A bug slips through because there is no gate. The strong condition is the same three properties filled in: page renders at 375px mobile width with no horizontal scroll; every link resolves on the actual deployment; change appears only in the expected files.

production_viz.label: weak vs strong — the worked pair
production_viz.mechanic: two-column table: left 'weak' shows three rows with vague entries in crimson; right 'strong' shows three rows with specific entries in teal; a bug icon passes through the left gate but is blocked by the right

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] weak vs strong — bug through left, blocked right

beats.narration_text: The condition that would have caught the syllabus-link bug: every link on the new page resolves on the actual deployment — not just on local dev. Run the lint against the live URL, not the local file. One command difference. Three-day bug avoided. The condition that catches the failure must check the same environment where the failure occurs.
beats.scene_description: a terminal showing npm run lint-html output with a link resolution check, pinned to newsprint, editorial collage, desaturated
beats.image_prompt: B06, printed terminal output showing npm run lint-html with a link check result — one line passes, one line shows a 404 resolution failure — pinned to aged newsprint, editorial collage style, highly desaturated, cream and charcoal tones
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] terminal showing lint-html link check

beats.narration_text: Here is where this compounds. The instinct when a handoff condition fails is to ask Claude to fix it. Claude corrects. Maybe it works. Maybe it adds a second wrong fix on top of the first. The session context now contains the failed step and its correction history. The next prompt runs against that pollution. After two failed corrections on the same step, the context is the problem — not the code.

production_viz.label: forward correction compounds — context accumulates failures
production_viz.mechanic: a conversation context bar filling with red segments: 'failed step', 'correction attempt 1', 'correction attempt 2'; a label showing context growing darker each step; an arrow labeled 'next prompt runs against failure history'

beats.narration_text: The structural fix is revert and respecify. When the handoff condition fails: slash-rewind to the checkpoint before the failed step. The failed step is gone from both Claude's memory and your file system. Then rewrite the specification with the failure as an explicit negative constraint. Run again from a clean state.

production_viz.label: revert-and-respecify: /rewind then add the failure as a constraint
production_viz.mechanic: a two-step flow: left '/rewind — failed step removed from context and files'; right 'respecify — add failure as negative constraint'; a clean-state label showing the session back at the checkpoint

beats.narration_text: The practical move: before approving any Claude Code build step, write the handoff condition in one sentence that names the artifact, the verification method, and the threshold. If automated, run it before approving. If the condition fails, use slash-rewind not forward correction. After two failures on the same step, clear and rewrite from scratch.

production_viz.label: three-row practical card: write condition; if fails, /rewind; two failures, /clear
production_viz.mechanic: three teal rows drawing in one at a time: 'Write the condition before approving' / 'Condition fails: /rewind + respecify' / 'Two failures: /clear + rewrite from scratch'

beats.narration_text: Exit zero is not a handoff condition. Looks good is not a handoff condition. The condition that catches the syllabus link bug is specific, testable, and binary — and it is written before the step runs, not discovered when the student emails.

card.copy: Not 'looks good.' A specific, testable, binary condition written before the step.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The practical move: before approving any Claude Code build step, write the handoff condition in one sentence that names the artifact, the verification method, and the threshold. If automated, run it before approving. If the condition fails, use slash-rewind not forward correction. After two failures on the same step, clear and rewrite from scratch. Exit zero is not a handoff condition. Looks good is not a handoff condition. The condition that catches the syllabus link bug is specific, testable, and binary — and it is written before the step runs, not discovered when the student emails.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why 'Looks Good' Fails as a Gate and What to Write Instead] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?

props.title: Why 'Looks Good' Fails as a Gate and What to Write Instead
